---
title: "Final Project: Beyond Location: Unpacking Airbnb Pricing with Machine Learning and Zipcode Intelligence"
author: "Shiyi Li"
toc: true
number-sections: true
highlight-style: pygments
format: 
  html: 
    code-fold: true
    html-math-method: katex
    embed-resources: true
    self-contained-math: true	
  pdf: 
    geometry: 
      - top=30mm
      - left=20mm
##  docx: Never, unless to accommodate a collaborator
cite-method: citeproc
link-citations: true
suppress-bibliography: false
bibliography: references.bib
---


a.  Dataset Cleaning
        i.  Import 12 monthly New York City Airbnb rental house listing datasets from 4/6/2024 to 3/1/2025.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Load the 12 monthly New York City Airbnb rental house listing datasets from 4/6/2024 to 3/1/2025
df1 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240406.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df2 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240503.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df3 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240604.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df4 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240705.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df5 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240805.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df6 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20240904.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df7 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20241004.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df8 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20241104.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df9 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20241204.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df10 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20250103.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df11 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20250201.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])
df12 = pd.read_csv('../data/listings_20250301.csv', 
                  dtype={'latitude': np.float32, 
                        'longitude': np.float32}, 
                  parse_dates=["last_scraped", 
                                "host_since", 
                                "calendar_updated", 
                                "calendar_last_scraped", 
                                "first_review", 
                                "last_review"])

# Combine the 12 datasets by rows
df_2024 = pd.concat([df1, df2, df3, df4, df5, df6, df7, df8, df9, df10, df11, df12], axis=0, ignore_index=True)

# Check the shape of the combined dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.shape)

# Check the last few rows
print(df_2024.tail(n=15))
```

        i. Rename the columns with our preferred styles.

```{python}
# Replace column names: convert to lowercase and replace spaces with underscores
df_2024.columns = df_2024.columns.str.lower().str.replace(' ', '_')
```

        i. Get the missing percentage of each variables. Remove the variables that are close to completely missing ( > 80% missing percentage ).

```{python}
# Check for the missing percentage of each variables
missing_percentage = (df_2024.isnull().sum()/df_2024.shape[0]*100).sort_values(ascending=False)

# Print the variables with missing percentage greater than 0
print(missing_percentage[missing_percentage > 0])

# Drop the variables that are close to completely missing
df_2024 = df_2024.drop(columns=list(missing_percentage[missing_percentage > 80].index))

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024, after drop the variables that are close to completely missing
df_2024.shape

# Check the frist few rows
print(df_2024.head(n=15))

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.info())
```

        i. Get the basic summaries of each variables: descriptive statistics for continuous variables; frequency tables for discrete variables.

```{python}
# List all of the continuous variables
continuous_variables = [column for column in df_2024.columns.drop(['id', 'scrape_id', 'host_id']) if df_2024[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the discrete variables
discrete_variables = [column for column in df_2024.columns.drop(continuous_variables)]

# Check the descriptive statistics for continuous variables
print(df_2024[df_2024.columns.drop(['id', 'scrape_id', 'host_id'])].describe())

# Check the frequency tables for discrete variables
for column in discrete_variables:
  print(f'Frequency tables for {column}')
  print(f'{df_2024[column].value_counts(dropna=False).reset_index()} \n\n')
```

        i. Drop the rows with any missing value in the specific variables/columns.

```{python}
# Drop the rows with any missing value in the specific variables/columns
df_2024.dropna(subset=['host_since', 'host_response_time', 'host_response_rate', 'host_acceptance_rate', 'host_is_superhost', 'host_total_listings_count', 'host_has_profile_pic', 'host_identity_verified', 'bathrooms_text', 'bedrooms', 'price', 'minimum_nights', 'maximum_nights', 'minimum_minimum_nights', 'maximum_minimum_nights', 'minimum_maximum_nights', 'maximum_maximum_nights', 'minimum_nights_avg_ntm', 'maximum_nights_avg_ntm', 'has_availability', 'last_review', 'review_scores_rating', 'review_scores_accuracy', 'review_scores_cleanliness', 'review_scores_checkin', 'review_scores_communication', 'review_scores_location', 'review_scores_value', 'reviews_per_month'], inplace=True)

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024
df_2024.shape

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.info())

# Need to remove the unnecessary variables: ['host_listings_count', 'neighbourhood_cleansed', 'first_review']
# Change the name of the 'neighbourhood_group_cleansed' variable to 'borough'.
```

        i. Change the name of the 'neighbourhood_group_cleansed' variable to 'borough'.

```{python}
# Change the name of the 'neighbourhood_group_cleansed' variable to 'borough'
df_2024.rename(columns={'neighbourhood_group_cleansed': 'borough'}, inplace=True)
```

        i. Create 'Neighborhood_highlights' variable by converting 'neighbourhood' to 'f' if 'neighbourhood' is null, otherwise 't', since 'neighbourhood' only has one possible value 'Neighborhood_highlights' and has 44.535060% missing values.

```{python}
# Create 'Neighborhood_highlights' variable by converting 'neighbourhood' to binary (NaN: 'f' & 'Neighborhood highlights': 't')
df_2024['Neighborhood_highlights'] = df_2024['neighbourhood'].isnull().map({True: 'f', False: 't'})

# Remove the 'neighbourhood' variable
df_2024 = df_2024.drop(columns=['neighbourhood'])
```

        i. Create a new 'bathrooms' variable by extract the number of bathrooms described in the 'bathrooms_text' variable, since the original 'bathrooms' variable has more missing values than the 'bathrooms_text' variable describing the number of bathrooms. Then, remove 'bathrooms_text' variable.

```{python}
# Define a function to return the first word of 'bathrooms_text' as float data type if the first word is a float, otherwise return np.nan.
def first_word_is_number(text):
    try:
        return float(text.split()[0])
    except (ValueError, IndexError):
        return np.nan

# Create a new 'bathrooms' variable
df_2024['bathrooms'] = df_2024['bathrooms_text'].apply(first_word_is_number)

# Drop the 'bathrooms_text' column
df_2024.drop(columns=['bathrooms_text'], inplace=True)

# Drop rows where 'bathrooms' is missing
df_2024.dropna(subset=['bathrooms'], inplace=True)

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.shape)
```

        i. Create 'bathroom_type' variable by comparing the number of bathrooms and the number of bedrooms from the variables 'bathrooms' and 'bedrooms'. If the number of bathrooms is smaller than the number of bedrooms, then the 'bathroom_type' has value of 'Shared bath', otherwise 'Private bath'.

```{python}
# Create a new 'bathroom_type' variable
df_2024['bathroom_type'] = df_2024[['bathrooms', 'bedrooms']].apply(lambda row: 'Shared bath' if row['bathrooms']<row['bedrooms'] else 'Private bath', axis=1)
```

        i. Check and adjust 'beds' if 'beds' is null or the number of beds in the 'beds' variable is wrong (Ex. nonzero 'bedrooms' with zero 'beds').

```{python}
# Check for zero 'beds' with nonzero 'bedrooms'
print(df_2024[(df_2024['beds'] == 0) & (df_2024['bedrooms'] > 0)][['beds', 'bedrooms', 'accommodates', 'room_type']])

# Adjust 'beds' by corresponding 'bedrooms' if 'beds' is null or has value of zero ('beds' = 'bedrooms').
df_2024.loc[(df_2024['beds'] == 0) | (df_2024['beds'].isnull()), 'beds'] = df_2024.loc[(df_2024['beds'] == 0) | (df_2024['beds'].isnull()), 'bedrooms']
```

        i. Create 'amenities_count' and 'host_verifications_count' variables by counting the number of elements in the list of 'amenities' and 'host_verifications' variables the data type of the 'amenities' variable to list (if len(value) > 2: len(x.split(',')) , otherwise 0)

```{python}
for column in ['amenities', 'host_verifications']:
        # Check if the variable is missing occur
        num_miss = df_2024[column].isnull().sum()
        print(f"There are {num_miss} rows missing '{column}' value.")

        # Create a count variable by counting the number of elements in the list value of the variable.
        df_2024[column+'_count'] = df_2024[column].apply(lambda x: len(x.split(',')) if len(x)>2 else 0)

        # Remove the original variable
        df_2024 = df_2024.drop(columns=[column])

        # Check the descriptive statistics for 'num_amenities' variables
        print(df_2024[column+'_count'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Convert 'host_response_rate' variable to numeric 

```{python}
# Check if 'host_response_rate' is missing occur
num_miss_host_response_rate = df_2024['host_response_rate'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {num_miss_host_response_rate} rows missing 'host_response_rate' value.")

# Convert 'host_response_rate' variable to numeric 
df_2024['host_response_rate'] = df_2024['host_response_rate'].apply(lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x[:-1]))

# Check the descriptive statistics for 'host_response_rate' variables
print(df_2024['host_response_rate'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Convert 'host_acceptance_rate' variable to numeric

```{python}
# Check if 'host_acceptance_rate' is missing occur
num_miss_host_acceptance_rate = df_2024['host_acceptance_rate'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {num_miss_host_acceptance_rate} rows missing 'host_acceptance_rate' value.")

# Convert 'host_acceptance_rate' variable to numeric 
df_2024['host_acceptance_rate'] = df_2024['host_acceptance_rate'].apply(lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x[:-1]))

# Check the descriptive statistics for 'host_acceptance_rate' variables
print(df_2024['host_acceptance_rate'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Convert 'price' variable to numeric

```{python}
# Check if 'price' is missing occur
num_miss_price = df_2024['price'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {num_miss_price} rows missing 'price' value.")

# Create a proper translation table to remove '$' and ',' characters
remove_chars = str.maketrans({'$': '', ',': ''})

# Convert 'price' variable to numeric 
df_2024['price'] = df_2024['price'].apply(lambda x: pd.to_numeric(x.translate(remove_chars)))

# Check the descriptive statistics for 'price' variables
print(df_2024['price'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Create 'last_review_since' by subtracting 'last_review' date from 'last_scraped' date and converting the unit to months.

```{python}
# Calculate time since last review in months
df_2024['last_review_since'] = (df_2024['last_scraped'] - df_2024['last_review']).dt.days / 30

# Drop 'last_review' variable/column
df_2024.drop(columns=['last_review'], inplace=True)
```

        i. Convert 'id', 'scrape_id', 'host_id' variables to string data type.

```{python}
# Convert 'id', 'scrape_id', 'host_id' variables to string data type.
id_columns = ['id', 'scrape_id', 'host_id']

for column in id_columns:
  df_2024[column] = df_2024[column].where(df_2024[column].isnull(), df_2024[column].astype(str))  # The where(condition, other) method replaces the values of the rows where the condition evaluates to False, otherwise if the condition is true, then replace the rows with 'other'.

# Check the data type of the variable
print(df_2024[id_columns].dtypes, '\n')
```

        i. Create 'name_sentiment', 'name_subjectivity', 'host_about_sentiment', 'host_about_subjectivity', 'description_sentiment', 'description_subjectivity', 'neighborhood_overview_sentiment', and 'neighborhood_overview_subjectivity' variables by using the Sentiment Analysis on the 'name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview' variables. (NaN: 0, for all the variables)

```{python}
from textblob import TextBlob

import nltk
nltk.download('averaged_perceptron_tagger')

for column in ['name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview']:

  # Check if the variable is missing occur
  num_miss_rows = df_2024[column].isnull().sum()
  print(f"There are {num_miss_rows} rows missing '{column}' value.")

  # Extract the sentiment score of the variable
  df_2024[column + '_sentiment'] = df_2024[column].apply(
      lambda x: TextBlob(str(x)).sentiment.polarity if pd.notnull(x) else 0
      )

  # Extract the subjective score of the variable
  df_2024[column + '_subjectivity'] = df_2024[column].apply(
      lambda x: TextBlob(str(x)).sentiment.subjectivity if pd.notnull(x) else 0
      )

  # Check the descriptive statistics for the sentiment variables
  print(df_2024[column + '_sentiment'].describe(), '\n')

  # Check the descriptive statistics for the subjective variables
  print(df_2024[column + '_subjectivity'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Convert 'name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview' variables to numeric by counting the number of words in each value, and store them as 'name_count', 'host_about_count', 'description_count', 'neighborhood_overview_count' variables. Remove the original variables, 'name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview'.

```{python}
# Check if 'name' is missing occur
num_miss_name = df_2024['name'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {num_miss_name} rows missing 'name' value.")

# Convert 'name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview' variables to numeric by counting the number of words in each value
for column in ['name', 'host_about', 'description', 'neighborhood_overview']:
  df_2024[column+'_count'] = df_2024[column].apply(lambda x: int(len(x.split())) if pd.notnull(x) else 0)

  # Drop the original variable
  df_2024.drop(columns=[column], inplace=True)

  # Check the descriptive statistics for the variables
  print(df_2024[column+'_count'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Create 'account_age' variable, describing that how long has the host had their Airbnb account, in years, by subtracting 'host_since' variable from ‘last_scraped’ variable and converting the unit to years. 

```{python}
# Calculate the account age in years and stored as 'account_age' variable
df_2024['account_age'] = (df_2024['last_scraped'] - df_2024['host_since']).dt.days / 365

# Check the descriptive statistics for 'account_age' variables
print(df_2024['account_age'].describe(), '\n')

# Drop the original variable, 'host_since'.
df_2024.drop(columns=['host_since'], inplace=True)
```

        i. Create 'host_info' variable by combining the informations from the variables 'host_id', 'host_url', 'host_location', 'host_thumbnail_url', 'host_picture_url', 'host_has_profile_pic', 'host_identity_verified', 'host_verifications', 'host_neighbourhood', 'host_about_count', 'host_name', 'account_age' variables. 

```{python}
# Create 'host_info' variable by summing up the 'account_age', np.log1p('host_about_count'), and 'host_verifications_count' variables
df_2024['host_info'] = (
    df_2024['account_age'] +
    np.log1p(df_2024['host_about_count']) +
    df_2024['host_verifications_count']
)

# Add 1 if host has profile pic or identity verified, otherwise add 0
for column in ['host_has_profile_pic', 'host_identity_verified']:
    df_2024['host_info'] += df_2024[column].apply(lambda x: 1 if x == 't' else 0)

# Add 1 if the variable is NOT null, otherwise add 0
for column in ['host_id', 'host_url', 'host_location', 'host_thumbnail_url', 'host_picture_url', 'host_neighbourhood', 'host_name']:
    df_2024['host_info'] += df_2024[column].apply(lambda x: 0 if pd.isnull(x) else 1)

# Check the descriptive statistics for the 'host_info' variable
print(df_2024['host_info'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Create 'listing_info' variable by combining the informations from the variables 'listing_url', 'name_count', 'description_count', 'neighborhood_overview_count', 'picture_url', variables.  

```{python}
# Create 'listing_info' variable by summing up the np.log1p(df_2024['name_count']), np.log1p(df_2024['description_count']), and np.log1p(df_2024['neighborhood_overview_count']) variables
df_2024['listing_info'] = (
    np.log1p(df_2024['name_count']) +
    np.log1p(df_2024['description_count']) +
    np.log1p(df_2024['neighborhood_overview_count'])
)

# Check the descriptive statistics for the 'listing_info' variable
print(df_2024['listing_info'].describe(), '\n')
```

        i. Create 'host_in_NYC' by extracting the city from 'host_location' variable (NYC: 't' & otherwise: 'f')

```{python}
# Create 'host_in_NYC' variable by extracting the first 2 words in the string value of the 'host_location' variable.
df_2024['host_in_NYC'] = df_2024['host_location'].apply(
    lambda x: 't' if isinstance(x, str) and x.lower().startswith('new york') else 'f'
)

# Check the number of unique values for the 'host_in_NYC' variable
print(df_2024['host_in_NYC'].value_counts(), '\n')

# Drop the original 'host_location' variable
df_2024.drop(columns=['host_location'], inplace=True)
```

        i. Drop the unnecessary variables 'id', 'listing_url', 'scrape_id', 'last_scraped', 'picture_url', 'host_id', 'host_url', 'host_name', 'host_thumbnail_url', 'host_picture_url', 'host_neighbourhood', 'calendar_last_scraped', 'host_listings_count', 'neighbourhood_cleansed', and 'first_review'.

```{python}
# Drop the redundant variable 'property_type'
df_2024 = df_2024.drop(columns=['id', 'listing_url', 'scrape_id', 'last_scraped', 'picture_url', 'host_id', 'host_url', 'host_name', 'host_thumbnail_url', 'host_picture_url', 'host_neighbourhood', 'calendar_last_scraped', 'host_listings_count', 'neighbourhood_cleansed', 'first_review'])
```

        i. Drop all the rows with any missing values.

```{python}
# Remove all the rows with any missing values
df_2024 = df_2024.dropna()

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.info())

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024, after removed all the rows with any missing values
print(df_2024.shape)
```

        i. Generate 'zip_code' variable using the geo codes. Since the dataset is too large and we want to merge zipcode level information from ACS data of the year of 2023, we choose to generate 'zip_code' variable using the official nationwide shapefile for ZIP Code Tabulation Areas (ZCTAs) from the U.S. Census Bureau for the year 2023.

```{python}
import requests
import zipfile
import os
import io

# Define the US ZIPCode shapefile URL, the official nationwide shapefile for ZIP Code Tabulation Areas (ZCTAs) from the U.S. Census Bureau for the year 2023, and extraction directory
shapefile_url = "https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/TIGER2023/ZCTA520/tl_2023_us_zcta520.zip"
extract_dir = "US_ZIPCode_Shapefile"

# Create the directory if it doesn't exist
os.makedirs(extract_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Step 2: Download and extract the ZIPCode_Shapefile
print("Downloading ZIPCode shapefile...")
response = requests.get(shapefile_url)
with zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(response.content), "r") as z:
    z.extractall(extract_dir)

print(f"ZIPCode_Shapefile extracted to: {extract_dir}")
```

```{python}
# Step 2: Automatically detect the correct .shp file
shapefile_path = None
for file in os.listdir(extract_dir):
    if file.endswith(".shp"):
        shapefile_path = os.path.join(extract_dir, file)
        break  # Use the first .shp file found

if not shapefile_path:
    raise FileNotFoundError("No .shp file found in extracted directory.")

print(f"Using shapefile: {shapefile_path}")
```

```{python}
import geopandas as gpd

# Step 3: Load the shapefile into GeoPandas
us_zipcode_shape = gpd.read_file(shapefile_path)

# Step 4: Convert to CRS with latitude/longitude (WGS 84) for correct mapping
us_zipcode_shape = us_zipcode_shape.to_crs(epsg=4326)

# Preview
us_zipcode_shape = us_zipcode_shape[['ZCTA5CE20', 'geometry']].rename(columns={'ZCTA5CE20': 'zip_code'})

print(us_zipcode_shape.head(n=10))
```

```{python}
from shapely.geometry import Point

# Check if missing values in geo codes occur
# Count rows where geo codes are missing
geo_missing = df_2024[['latitude', 'longitude']].isnull().all(axis=1).sum()
print(f"There are {geo_missing} rows where geo codes are missing")

# Filter valid lat/lon pairs
geo_df = df_2024[['latitude', 'longitude']].copy()

# Create geometry points
geo_df['geometry'] = geo_df.apply(lambda row: Point(row['longitude'], row['latitude']), axis=1)

# Convert to GeoDataFrame
# Make sure both datasets are in the same coordinate system (WGS 84; epsg=4326)
geo_df = gpd.GeoDataFrame(geo_df, geometry='geometry', crs="EPSG:4326")
```

```{python}
# Add index to keep track of rows
geo_df['orig_index'] = geo_df.index

# Perform spatial join
matched = gpd.sjoin(geo_df, us_zipcode_shape, how='left', predicate='within')

# Drop duplicates if any (based on original index)
matched = matched.drop_duplicates(subset='orig_index')

# Now `matched['zip_code']` contains the ZIP code for each coordinate
```

```{python}
# Merge back on index instead of lat/lon
df_2024['zip_code'] = matched.set_index('orig_index')['zip_code']

# Check if missing values in 'zip_code' occur
# Count rows where 'zip_code' are missing
zipcode_missing = df_2024['zip_code'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {zipcode_missing} rows where 'zip_code' are missing")
```

        i. Check if there is any invalid NYC zipcode.

```{python}
# Check if there is any 'zip_code' without 5 digits.
rows_not_5_digits = df_2024[
        ~df_2024['zip_code'].astype(str).str.match(r'^\d{5}$')]['zip_code'].count()
print(f"There are {rows_not_5_digits} rows where 'zip_code' without 5 digits.")
```

```{python}
# List of valid NYC ZIP codes compiled from UHF codes
# Define all_valid_zips based on the earlier extracted ZIP codes
all_valid_zips = {
    10463, 10471, 10466, 10469, 10470, 10475, 10458, 10467, 10468,
    10461, 10462, 10464, 10465, 10472, 10473, 10453, 10457, 10460,
    10451, 10452, 10456, 10454, 10455, 10459, 10474, 11211, 11222,
    11201, 11205, 11215, 11217, 11231, 11213, 11212, 11216, 11233,
    11238, 11207, 11208, 11220, 11232, 11204, 11218, 11219, 11230,
    11203, 11210, 11225, 11226, 11234, 11236, 11239, 11209, 11214,
    11228, 11223, 11224, 11229, 11235, 11206, 11221, 11237, 10031,
    10032, 10033, 10034, 10040, 10026, 10027, 10030, 10037, 10039,
    10029, 10035, 10023, 10024, 10025, 10021, 10028, 10044, 10128,
    10001, 10011, 10018, 10019, 10020, 10036, 10010, 10016, 10017,
    10022, 10012, 10013, 10014, 10002, 10003, 10009, 10004, 10005,
    10006, 10007, 10038, 10280, 11101, 11102, 11103, 11104, 11105,
    11106, 11368, 11369, 11370, 11372, 11373, 11377, 11378, 11354,
    11355, 11356, 11357, 11358, 11359, 11360, 11361, 11362, 11363,
    11364, 11374, 11375, 11379, 11385, 11365, 11366, 11367, 11414,
    11415, 11416, 11417, 11418, 11419, 11420, 11421, 11412, 11423,
    11432, 11433, 11434, 11435, 11436, 11004, 11005, 11411, 11413,
    11422, 11426, 11427, 11428, 11429, 11691, 11692, 11693, 11694,
    11695, 11697, 10302, 10303, 10310, 10301, 10304, 10305, 10314,
    10306, 10307, 10308, 10309, 10312
}

# Convert ZIP codes to numeric, invalid entries become NaN
df_2024['zip_code_numeric'] = pd.to_numeric(df_2024['zip_code'], errors='coerce')

# Identify invalid ZIP codes (including NaN)
invalid_zips = df_2024[
    df_2024['zip_code_numeric'].isna() | 
    ~df_2024['zip_code_numeric'].isin(all_valid_zips)
    ]['zip_code']

# Calculate frequency of invalid ZIP codes
invalid_zip_freq = invalid_zips.value_counts(dropna=False).reset_index()
invalid_zip_freq.columns = ['zip_code', 'frequency']

print(invalid_zip_freq)

print(f"\nTotal number of invalid 'zip_code' values: "
      f"{invalid_zip_freq['frequency'].sum()}")

print(f"\nTotal number of illegal NYC 'zip_code' values: "
      f"{invalid_zip_freq[1:]['frequency'].sum()}")
```

```{python}
# Replace invalid NYC zip codes with `NA`
df_2024.loc[
    df_2024['zip_code_numeric'].isna() | 
    ~df_2024['zip_code_numeric'].isin(all_valid_zips), 'zip_code'] = np.nan

# Drop the 'zip_code_numeric' column
df_2024 = df_2024.drop(columns=['zip_code_numeric'])
```

        i. Check if there is any missing value in 'borough' variable.

```{python}
# Check if missing values in 'borough' occur
# Count rows where 'borough' are missing
borough_missing = df_2024['borough'].isnull().sum()
print(f"There are {borough_missing} rows where 'borough' are missing")
```

        i. Drop all the rows with any missing values.

```{python}
# Remove all the rows with any missing values
df_2024 = df_2024.dropna()

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024.info())

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024, after removed all the rows with any missing values
print(df_2024.shape)
```


a.  Exploratory analysis.

        i. Overlay the locations of the listings on a map of NYC and visualize the average price by zipcode on a NYC choropleth map. 

```{python}
# Load Required Libraries
import os
import io
import zipfile
import requests
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Define the NYC MODZCTA shapefile URL and extraction directory
shapefile_url = "https://data.cityofnewyork.us/api/geospatial/pri4-ifjk?method=export&format=Shapefile"
extract_dir = "MODZCTA_Shapefile"

# Create the directory if it doesn't exist
os.makedirs(extract_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Step 1: Download and extract the shapefile
print("Downloading MODZCTA shapefile...")
response = requests.get(shapefile_url)
with zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(response.content), "r") as z:
    z.extractall(extract_dir)

print(f"Shapefile extracted to: {extract_dir}")
```

```{python}
# Step 2: Automatically detect the correct .shp file
shapefile_path = None
for file in os.listdir(extract_dir):
    if file.endswith(".shp"):
        shapefile_path = os.path.join(extract_dir, file)
        break  # Use the first .shp file found

if not shapefile_path:
    raise FileNotFoundError("No .shp file found in extracted directory.")

print(f"Using shapefile: {shapefile_path}")

# Step 3: Load the shapefile into GeoPandas
gdf_nyc = gpd.read_file(shapefile_path)

# Step 4: Convert to CRS with latitude/longitude (WGS 84) for correct mapping
gdf_nyc = gdf_nyc.to_crs(epsg=4326)

print(gdf_nyc.head())
```

```{python}
# Check if missing values in geo codes occur
# Count rows where geo codes are missing
geo_missing = df_2024[['latitude', 'longitude']].isnull().all(axis=1).sum()
print(f"There are {geo_missing} rows where geo codes are missing")

# Remove missing values for latitude and longitude
df_listing = df_2024.dropna(subset=['latitude', 'longitude'])

# Convert to GeoDataFrame
gdf_listing = gpd.GeoDataFrame(df_listing, geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(
                                df_listing.longitude, 
                                df_listing.latitude
                                ), crs="EPSG:4326")

print(gdf_listing[['price', 'zip_code', 'latitude', 'longitude']])
```

```{python}
# Set up figure and axis
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 12))

# Plot NYC boundary map
gdf_nyc.plot(ax=ax, cmap='viridis', 
linewidth=0.8, 
edgecolor='black',
legend=True, 
label="NYC Boundaries")

# Overlay listing locations as red dots
gdf_listing.plot(
  ax=ax, color='pink', markersize=2, alpha=0.6, label="listing Locations"
  )

# Add title and legend
ax.set_title("Airbnb NYC listing Locations Overlay on NYC Map", fontsize=14)
ax.legend(loc="lower right", fontsize=13)

# Remove axes
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_frame_on(False)

# Show plot
plt.show()
```

```{python}
# Calculate the mean price for each zip_code area in dollars.
avg_price_by_zipcode = gdf_listing.groupby('zip_code')['price'].mean().reset_index()

#  Merge 'avg_price_by_zipcode' directly with modzcta land area (`gdf_nyc`)
gdf_nyc = gdf_nyc.merge(avg_price_by_zipcode, how='left', left_on='modzcta', right_on='zip_code')

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 12))
gdf_nyc.plot(
    column='price',
    cmap="OrRd",
    linewidth=0.8,
    edgecolor='black',
    legend=True,
    legend_kwds={
        'label': "Average Airbnb Price ($)",
        'orientation': "horizontal"
    },
    ax=ax
)

ax.set_title("Average Airbnb Price by Zipcode in NYC", fontsize=14)
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_frame_on(False)
plt.show()
```

Conclusion: From these two NYC map plot, we can see the zipcode area with more listings avaliable has a higher average price for the listings, especially high average price in the borough, Manhattan and Brooklyn.

```{python}
# Check for the value counts in the 'zip_code' variable
print(df_2024['zip_code'].value_counts())

# Check for the value counts in the 'borough' variable
print(df_2024['borough'].value_counts())
```

```{python}
# Count the number of listings in each zip_code area.
listing_count_by_zipcode = df_2024['zip_code'].value_counts().reset_index()

#  Merge 'listing_count_by_zipcode' directly with 'zip_code' variable in the dataframe, df_2024
df_2024 = df_2024.merge(listing_count_by_zipcode, how='left', left_on='zip_code', right_on='zip_code')

# Change the name of the 'count' variable to 'num_listing_by_zipcode'
df_2024.rename(columns={'count': 'num_listing_by_zipcode'}, inplace=True)

# Check the descriptive statistics for the 'num_listing_by_zipcode' variable
print(df_2024['num_listing_by_zipcode'].describe(), '\n')

df_2024.info()
```

###### 需要回来继续分析！！！！！！！！！！！

        i. Does the target, `price`, depende on `num_listing_by_zipcode`?

```{python}
import statsmodels.api as sm

X_num_listing = df_2024['num_listing_by_zipcode']
X_num_listing = sm.add_constant(X_num_listing)
y_price = df_2024['price']  # or log1p(price)

SLR_model = sm.OLS(y_price, X_num_listing).fit()
print(SLR_model.summary(), '\n')

p_value = SLR_model.pvalues['num_listing_by_zipcode']

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant relationship between the target, `price`, and the `num_listing_by_zipcode`. Therefore, we can conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the `num_listing_by_zipcode`.\n")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates there is no significant relationship between the target, `price`, and the `num_listing_by_zipcode`. Therefore, we can conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the different `num_listing_by_zipcode`.\n")
```

        i. Does the target, `price`, depende on different boroughs?

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

filtered_df = df_2024.copy()
filtered_df['log(price)'] = np.log1p(filtered_df['price'])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=filtered_df, x='borough', y='log(price)')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.title('log(Price) Distribution by Borough')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Use One-Way ANOVA to test if the mean price is significantly different across boroughs. 
# If p < 0.05: There is a significant difference in price across boroughs. = Ha
# If p >= 0.05: No significant difference detected. = H0


# Group prices by boroughs
groups = [group["price"].values for name, group in df_2024.groupby("borough")]

# Run ANOVA
f_stat, p_value = stats.f_oneway(*groups)
print(f"F-statistic: {f_stat:.2f}, p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different boroughs. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different boroughs.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different boroughs. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the different boroughs.")
```

        i. Does the target, `price`, depende on different property types, `property_type`?

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Filter out property types with very few listings if needed
# top_types = df_2024['property_type'].value_counts().head(10).index
filtered_df = df_2024.copy()

filtered_df['log(price)'] = np.log1p(filtered_df['price'])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=filtered_df, x='property_type', y='log(price)')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title('log(Price) Distribution by Property Type')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Use One-Way ANOVA to test if the mean price is significantly different across property types. 
# If p < 0.05: There is a significant difference in price across property types. = Ha
# If p >= 0.05: No significant difference detected. = H0


# Filter out property types with very few listings if needed
#top_types = df_2024['property_type'].value_counts().head(10).index
filtered_df = df_2024.copy()

# filtered_df['log(price)'] = np.log1p(filtered_df['price'])

# Group prices by property_type
groups = [group['price'].values for name, group in filtered_df.groupby("property_type")]

# Run ANOVA
f_stat, p_value = stats.f_oneway(*groups)
print(f"F-statistic: {f_stat:.2f}, p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different property types. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different property types.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different property types. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the different property types.")
```

```{python}
#| eval: false

from statsmodels.stats.multicomp import pairwise_tukeyhsd

# Step 1: Run Tukey HSD test to examine whether average prices significantly differ between each pair of property types.
# Step 1: Run Tukey HSD test
tukey_result = pairwise_tukeyhsd(endog=df_2024['price'], groups=df_2024['property_type'], alpha=0.05)

# Step 2: Convert summary to a DataFrame
tukey_df = pd.DataFrame(data=tukey_result._results_table.data[1:], columns=tukey_result._results_table.data[0])

# Step 3: Filter rows where reject == True (i.e., significant)
significant_diffs = tukey_df[tukey_df['reject'] == True]

# Sort by mean difference (ascending)
sorted_significant_diffs = significant_diffs.sort_values(by='meandiff', ascending=False)

# Optional: Display the result
print(sorted_significant_diffs.head(50))

# Step: Get all unique property types that were in significant comparisons
significant_groups = pd.unique(
    pd.concat([sorted_significant_diffs['group1'], sorted_significant_diffs['group2']])
)

print(f"Total unique property types with significant price differences: {len(significant_groups)}")
print(significant_groups)
```

        i. Does the target, `price`, depende on different room types, `room_type`?

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

filtered_df = df_2024.copy()

filtered_df['log(price)'] = np.log1p(filtered_df['price'])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=filtered_df, x='room_type', y='log(price)')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.title('log(Price) Distribution by Roomy Type')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Use One-Way ANOVA to test if the mean price is significantly different across room types. 
# If p < 0.05: There is a significant difference in price across room types. = Ha
# If p >= 0.05: No significant difference detected. = H0


# Group prices by room_type
groups = [group["price"].values for name, group in df_2024.groupby("room_type")]

# Run ANOVA
f_stat, p_value = stats.f_oneway(*groups)
print(f"F-statistic: {f_stat:.2f}, p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different room types. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different room types.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different room types. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the different room types.")
```

        i. Does the target, `price`, depende on the `host_response_time`?

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

filtered_df = df_2024.copy()

filtered_df['log(price)'] = np.log1p(filtered_df['price'])

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

# Boxplot showing distribution
sns.boxplot(data=filtered_df, x='host_response_time', y='log(price)')

# Overlay the mean points
sns.pointplot(data=filtered_df, x='host_response_time', y='log(price)', 
              estimator=np.mean, color='red', markers='D', linestyles='', ci=None)

plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.title('log(Price) Distribution by host_response_time')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Use One-Way ANOVA to test if the mean price is significantly different across 'host_response_time'. 
# If p < 0.05: There is a significant difference in price across host_response_time. = Ha
# If p >= 0.05: No significant difference detected. = H0


# Group prices by host_response_time
groups = [group["price"].values for name, group in df_2024.groupby("host_response_time")]

# Run ANOVA
f_stat, p_value = stats.f_oneway(*groups)
print(f"F-statistic: {f_stat:.2f}, p-value: {p_value:.4f}")

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different `host_response_time`. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different `host_response_time`.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different `host_response_time`. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the different `host_response_time`.")
```

        i.  Merge the listing data (df_2024) with the Census zip code database which
   contains zip-code level demographic or socioeconomic variables.

```{python}
# Import modules
from census import Census
from us import states

api_key = open("censusAPIkey.txt").read().strip()
c = Census(api_key)
```

```{python}
ACS_YEAR = 2023
ACS_DATASET = "acs/acs5"

# Important ACS variables (including land area for density calculation)
ACS_VARIABLES = {
    "B01003_001E": "Total Population",
    "B19013_001E": "Median Household Income",
    "B02001_002E": "White Population",
    "B02001_003E": "Black Population",
    "B02001_005E": "Asian Population",
    "B15003_022E": "Bachelor’s Degree Holders",
    "B15003_025E": "Graduate Degree Holders",
    "B23025_002E": "Labor Force",
    "B23025_005E": "Unemployed",
    "B25077_001E": "Median Home Value"
}

# Convert set to list of strings
all_valid_zips = list(map(str, all_valid_zips))
```

```{python}
acs_data = c.acs5.get(
    list(ACS_VARIABLES.keys()), 
    {'for': f'zip code tabulation area:{",".join(all_valid_zips)}'}
    )

# Convert to DataFrame
df_acs = pd.DataFrame(acs_data)

# Rename columns
df_acs.rename(columns=ACS_VARIABLES, inplace=True)
df_acs.rename(columns={"zip code tabulation area": "ZIP Code"}, inplace=True)

# Check the data types of the columns in 'df_acs'
print(df_acs.dtypes)

# Check the data types of the columns in 'df'
print(df_2024.dtypes)

# Merge listing data (df_2024) with Census data using ZIP Code as the key
df_2024_merged = df_2024.merge(
  df_acs, left_on='zip_code', right_on='ZIP Code', how='left'
  )

# Count missing census data
missing_census_data = df_2024_merged[df_2024_merged['Total Population'].isnull()]
print("Number of crashes with missing census data:", len(missing_census_data))

# Drop the redundant 'ZIP Code' variable from the dataset, df_2024_merged
df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['ZIP Code'], inplace=True)

# Check the merged dataframe
print(df_2024_merged.head(10))

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024_merged
print(df_2024_merged.info())
```

        i.  Merge the listing data (df_2024_merged) with the land area data in NYC MODZCTA shapefile from NYC Open Data.

```{python}
import requests
import zipfile
import geopandas as gpd

# Define the NYC MODZCTA shapefile URL and extraction directory
shapefile_url = "https://data.cityofnewyork.us/api/geospatial/pri4-ifjk?method=export&format=Shapefile"
extract_dir = "MODZCTA_Shapefile"

# Create the directory if it doesn't exist
os.makedirs(extract_dir, exist_ok=True)

# Step 1: Download and extract the shapefile
print("Downloading MODZCTA shapefile...")
response = requests.get(shapefile_url)
with zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(response.content), "r") as z:
    z.extractall(extract_dir)

print(f"Shapefile extracted to: {extract_dir}")
```

```{python}
# Step 2: Automatically detect the correct .shp file
shapefile_path = None
for file in os.listdir(extract_dir):
    if file.endswith(".shp"):
        shapefile_path = os.path.join(extract_dir, file)
        break  # Use the first .shp file found

if not shapefile_path:
    raise FileNotFoundError("No .shp file found in extracted directory.")

print(f"Using shapefile: {shapefile_path}")

# Step 3: Load the shapefile into GeoPandas
gdf = gpd.read_file(shapefile_path)

# Step 4: Convert to CRS with meters for accurate area calculation
gdf = gdf.to_crs(epsg=3857)

# Step 5: Compute land area in square miles
gdf['land_area_sq_miles'] = gdf['geometry'].area / 2_589_988.11
# 1 square mile = 2,589,988.11 square meters

print(gdf[['modzcta', 'land_area_sq_miles']].head())

gdf = gdf[['modzcta', 'land_area_sq_miles']]
```

```{python}
# Merge the listing data (df_2024_merged) directly with MODZCTA land area (`gdf`)
df_2024_merged = df_2024_merged.merge(gdf, left_on='zip_code', right_on='modzcta', how='left')

# Calculate Population Density (number of people per square mile in each zipcode area)
df_2024_merged['popdensity_per_sq_mile'] = (
    df_2024_merged['Total Population'] / df_2024_merged['land_area_sq_miles']
    )

# Display first few rows
print(df_2024_merged[['zip_code', 'Total Population', 'land_area_sq_miles',
    'popdensity_per_sq_mile']].head())

# Drop the redundant 'modzcta', 'land_area_sq_miles', and 'Total Population' variables from the dataset, df_2024_merged
df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['modzcta', 'land_area_sq_miles', 'Total Population'], inplace=True)
```

       i. Rename the columns with our preferred styles.

```{python}
# Replace column names: convert to lowercase and replace spaces with underscores
df_2024_merged.columns = df_2024_merged.columns.str.lower().str.replace(' ', '_')
```

        i. Drop all the rows with any missing values.

```{python}
# Remove all the rows with any missing values
df_2024_merged = df_2024_merged.dropna()

# Check the infomation of the dataset, df_2024
print(df_2024_merged.info())

# Check the shape of the dataset, df_2024, after removed all the rows with any missing values
print(df_2024_merged.shape)
```

        i. Does 'price' depende on 'popdensity_per_sq_mile' (number of people per square mile) ? (Using simple linear regression, t-test.)

```{python}
import statsmodels.api as sm

X_popdensity = df_2024_merged['popdensity_per_sq_mile']
X_popdensity = sm.add_constant(X_popdensity)
y_price = df_2024_merged['price']  # or log1p(price)

SLR_model = sm.OLS(y_price, X_popdensity).fit()
print(SLR_model.summary())

p_value = SLR_model.pvalues['popdensity_per_sq_mile']

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different 'popdensity_per_sq_mile'. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different 'popdensity_per_sq_mile'.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different 'popdensity_per_sq_mile'. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the predictor 'popdensity_per_sq_mile'.")
```

        i. Does 'price' depende on `median_household_income`? (Using simple linear regression, t-test.)

```{python}
import statsmodels.api as sm

X_income = df_2024_merged['median_household_income']
X_income = sm.add_constant(X_income)
y_price = df_2024_merged['price']  # or log1p(price)

SLR_model = sm.OLS(y_price, X_income).fit()
print(SLR_model.summary())

p_value = SLR_model.pvalues['median_household_income']

# Interpret results
if p_value < 0.05:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is smaller than alpha (0.05), we reject the null hypothesis. This indicates a significant difference in the target, `price`, between different `median_household_income`. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, depends on the different `median_household_income`.")
else:
  print(f"Because the p-value ({p_value}) is greater than alpha (0.05), we fail to reject the null hypothesis. This indicates no significant difference in the target, `price`, between different `median_household_income`. Therefore, we conclude that the target, `price`, is independent of the predictor `median_household_income`.")
```

        i. Merge 犯罪数据

        i. 提取交通便利变量

        i. 处理‘property_type’ variable. It has too much unique values. It's data type is string.



a.  Modeling Airbnb price of listings in NYC.

        i.  Randomly select 20% of the listings as testing data and 80% of the listings with seeds `1234`. Build a XGBoost model to predict `price` for
      the listings with the training data. (Use RandomizedSearchCV to tune parameters but not GridSearchCV because our dataset is larger. It will be very time comsuming to use the GridSearchCV tool in this dataset. Then, we use 10-fold cross validation to do the final model evaluation/reporting after tuning.)

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import xgboost as xgb
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, RandomizedSearchCV, cross_validate
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from xgboost import XGBRegressor
from scipy.stats import uniform, randint

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = df_XGBoot_model['price']  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Define transformations for categorical variable
categorical_transformer = OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore', drop='first')

# Define transformations for continuous variables
numerical_transformer = StandardScaler()

# Use ColumnTransformer to transform the columns
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ('cat', categorical_transformer, categorical_cols),
        ('num', numerical_transformer, numerical_cols)
    ]
)

# Split the data into training and testing sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Define a pipeline model, which preprocess the data and then fits a XGBoost model.
XGBoost_model = Pipeline(steps=[
    ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', XGBRegressor(
        objective='reg:squarederror', # Regression using squared error (L2 loss). It means the model is trying to predict a continuous numeric value (like price) and minimize the sum of squared differences between predictions and actual values. This is the default for regression tasks in XGBoost.
        eval_metric='rmse', 
        random_state=1234
        ))
])
```

```{python}
# Set hyperparameter search space
param_dist = {
    'regressor__n_estimators': randint(100, 300),
    'regressor__max_depth': randint(3, 10),
    'regressor__learning_rate': uniform(0.01, 0.3),
    'regressor__subsample': uniform(0.7, 0.3),       # [0.7, 1.0]
    'regressor__colsample_bytree': uniform(0.6, 0.4) # [0.6, 1.0]
}

# Run 30 random combinations of hyperparameters
random_search = RandomizedSearchCV(
    estimator=XGBoost_model,
    param_distributions=param_dist,
    n_iter=30,                       # You can increase to 50 or 100 for better tuning
    cv=10,                           # 10-fold cross-validation
    scoring='neg_mean_squared_error',
    n_jobs=-1,                       # Use all CPU cores
    verbose=1,
    random_state=1234
)

# Fit the model to the train dataset (X_train, y_train)
random_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Best pipeline (preprocessing + tuned model)
best_model = random_search.best_estimator_
```

```{python}
# Predict 'price' on the test dataset
y_pred = best_model.predict(X_test)

# Compute RMSE and R²
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Print best parameters and performance
print("Best Parameters Found:")
print(random_search.best_params_)
print(f"\n MSE (on test data): ${np.square(rmse):.2f}")
print(f"\n RMSE (on test data): ${rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score: {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
# Access trained booster from best_model
booster = best_model.named_steps['regressor']
fitted_preprocessor = best_model.named_steps['preprocessor']

# Try to set readable feature names
try:
    booster.get_booster().feature_names = list(fitted_preprocessor.get_feature_names_out())
except:
    print("Could not set feature names from preprocessor — continuing with default names.")

# Plot
xgb.plot_importance(booster, max_num_features=15, importance_type='gain')
plt.title("Top 15 Feature Importances (XGBoost)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Result Interpretation: We trained a machine learning model (XGBoost) to predict Airbnb listing prices across NYC based on property features like room type, location, and reviews. We fitted 10 folds for each of 30 random combinations of hyperparameters, totalling 300 fits, and found our best combination of hyperparameters (n_estimators = 275, max_depth = 9, learning_rate = 0.22, subsample = 0.72, colsample_bytree = 0.87), which has the lowest RMSE and highest R^2 between the 300 fits. From the result, on average, the model's price predictions are within about $59 of the actual Airbnb prices. It explains 92% of the variation in price — that’s a very strong model!

        i. Randomly select 20% of the listings as testing data and 80% of the listings with seeds `1234`. Build an MLR model using the same predictors as the XGBoost model to predict `price` for the listings and train the MLR model on the 80% training data.

```{python}
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = df_XGBoot_model['price']  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Define transformations for categorical variable
categorical_transformer = OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown='ignore', drop='first')

# Define transformations for continuous variables
numerical_transformer = StandardScaler()

# Use ColumnTransformer to transform the columns
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ('cat', categorical_transformer, categorical_cols),
        ('num', numerical_transformer, numerical_cols)
    ]
)

# Split the data into training and testing sets (在preprocessing the data 之前split train-test data，是因为standardizing，StandardScaler(), 是将数据集看作一个整体来进行standardizing 的。)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Define a pipeline model, which preprocess the data and then fits a XGBoost model.
MLR_model = Pipeline(steps=[
    ('preprocessor', preprocessor),
    ('regressor', LinearRegression())
])

# Fit the model to the train dataset (X_train, y_train)
MLR_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

```{python}
# Predict 'price' on the test dataset
y_pred = MLR_model.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R²
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Print MLR Model performance
print(f"MSE: {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE: {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score: {r2:.4f}")
```

        i. Build an OLS MLR model and check for Outliers or Skewed Distribution in the target, `price`.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = df_XGBoot_model['price']  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Standardize *only* numerical columns (keep dummy variables untouched)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X[numerical_cols])

# Add intercept for OLS Regression
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Fit/train the model on 80% training data
MLR_model = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()
```

```{python}
# Print full summary of the MLR_model result
print(MLR_model.summary())
#print(summarize(MLR_model))

# Predict 'price' on the 20% test data
y_pred = MLR_model.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R²
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Print MLR Model performance
print(f"MSE (on test data): {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE (on test data): {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score: {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Plot the distribution of price
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.histplot(df_XGBoot_model['price'], bins=100, kde=True)
plt.title('Histogram of Price')
plt.xlabel('Price')
plt.ylabel('Count')
plt.show()

# Plot the boxplot of price
plt.figure(figsize=(8,2))
sns.boxplot(x=df_XGBoot_model['price'])
plt.title('Boxplot of Price')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Calculate residuals
residuals = y_test - y_pred

# Plot the Q-Q Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
sm.qqplot(residuals, line='45', fit=True)
plt.title("Q-Q Plot of Residuals")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
from scipy.stats import shapiro

stat, p = shapiro(residuals)
print(f"Shapiro-Wilk Test p-value: {p:.4f}")

if p > 0.05:
    print("Residuals likely follow normal distribution.")
else:
    print("Residuals deviate from normality.")
```

Conclusion: The histogram of price shows that there is servesly right skewness on the distributiion of price. The boxplot of price shows that there are many outliers in the target, `price`. The normal QQ plot shows servesly departure from a straight line, and the Shapiro-Wilk test yields a p-value of 0.0000, suggesting that the normality assumption may not be valid in this MLR model. Therefore, log transformation on the target, `price`, was used to improve this MLR model. 

        i. Apply log transformation on `price`. Print the summary results of the MLR model using log(price). Then, interpret the result:

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Standardize *only* numerical columns (keep dummy variables untouched)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X[numerical_cols])

# Add intercept for OLS Regression
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Fit/train the model on 80% training data
MLR_model = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()
```

```{python}
# Print full summary of the MLR_model result
print(MLR_model.summary())
#print(summarize(MLR_model))

# Predict 'price' on the 20% test data
y_pred = MLR_model.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R²
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Print MLR Model performance
print(f"MSE (on test data): {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE (on test data): {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score: {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Plot the distribution of log(price)
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
sns.histplot(np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price']), bins=100, kde=True)
plt.title('Log-Transformed Price Distribution')
plt.xlabel('Log(Price + 1)')
plt.show()
```

```{python}
import scipy.stats as stats

# Calculate residuals
residuals = y_test - y_pred

# Plot the Q-Q Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
sm.qqplot(residuals, line='45', fit=True)
plt.title("Q-Q Plot of Residuals")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

```{python}
from scipy.stats import shapiro

stat, p = shapiro(residuals)
print(f"Shapiro-Wilk Test p-value: {p:.4f}")

if p > 0.05:
    print("Residuals likely follow normal distribution.")
else:
    print("Residuals deviate from normality.")
```

The histogram of log(price) and the normal Q–Q plot indicate that applying a log transformation to the target variable, price, improves its approximation to a normal distribution.

        i. Apply a log transformation of the target, `price`; Build a OLS model; Then check for the multicollinearity problem in this MLR model.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Standardize *only* numerical columns (keep dummy variables untouched)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X[numerical_cols])

# Add intercept for OLS Regression
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Fit/train the model on 80% training data
MLR_model = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()
```

```{python}
# Print full summary of the MLR_model result
print(MLR_model.summary())
#print(summarize(MLR_model))

# Predict 'price' on the 20% test data
y_pred = MLR_model.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R²
mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
rmse = np.sqrt(mse)
r2 = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)

# Print MLR Model performance
print(f"MSE (on test data): {mse:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE (on test data): {rmse:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score: {r2:.4f}")
```

```{python}
from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import pandas as pd

# Ensure X_train is a DataFrame
X_vif = X_train.copy()

# Drop the intercept column if it exists
if 'const' in X_vif.columns:
    X_vif = X_vif.drop(columns='const')

# Calculate VIF for each feature
vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
vif_data['feature'] = X_vif.columns
vif_data['VIF'] = [variance_inflation_factor(X_vif.values, i) for i in range(X_vif.shape[1])]

# Sort by VIF value in descending order
vif_data = vif_data.sort_values(by='VIF', ascending=False)
print(vif_data[vif_data["VIF"]>5], '\n')
print(f"There are total {vif_data[vif_data["VIF"]>5].shape[0]} predictors with a VIF value greater than 5.\n")

# Sort by VIF value in ascending order
vif_data = vif_data.sort_values(by='VIF', ascending=True)
print(vif_data[vif_data["VIF"]<=5],)
print(f"There are total {vif_data[vif_data["VIF"]<=5].shape[0]} predictors with a VIF value less than or equal to 5.\n")
```

Conclusion: The Multicollinearity (VIF > 5) table shows that there are 85 out of 138 predictors with a VIF value greater than 5, which indicates that there are strong multicollinearity problems in this MLR model. Therefore, we use Ridge Regression (L2), a regularisation technique, to improve this model, because the number of predictors is smaller than the number of observations.

       i. Apply a log transformation of the target, `price` and the Ridge Regression (L2) technique to the MLR model.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Standardize *only* numerical columns (keep dummy variables untouched)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X[numerical_cols])

# Add intercept for OLS Regression
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)
```

```{python}
from sklearn.linear_model import RidgeCV

# Fit multiple linear regression with L2 regularization (Ridge) on training data
ridge_cv = RidgeCV(alphas=np.logspace(-6, 6, 100), cv=10)
ridge_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict on test set
y_pred_ridge = ridge_cv.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R^2 score
mse_ridge = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_ridge)
rmse_ridge = np.sqrt(mse_ridge)
r2_ridge = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_ridge)

# Print the results
print("Best alpha (Ridge):", ridge_cv.alpha_)
print(f"Ridge MSE (on test data): {mse_ridge:.2f}")
print(f"Ridge RMSE (on test data): {rmse_ridge:.2f}")
print(f"Ridge R² Score: {r2_ridge:.4f}")
```

        i. Apply a log transformation of the target, `price`, the Elastic net Regression (L1 + L2) technique to the MLR model. 

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from ISLP.models import (ModelSpec as MS,
                         summarize,
                         poly)
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Add constant (column: intercept=1) column into X
design = MS(X.columns)
X = design.fit_transform(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)
```

```{python}
from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNetCV

# Fit multiple linear regression with L1 + L2 regularization (Elastic net: A mix of Lasso and Ridge) on training data
elastic_cv = ElasticNetCV(l1_ratio=[.1, .5, .9], alphas=np.logspace(-6, 2, 100),
                          cv=5, max_iter=10000, random_state=42)
elastic_cv.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict on test set
y_pred_elastic = elastic_cv.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R^2 score
mse_elastic = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_elastic)
rmse_elastic = np.sqrt(mse_elastic)
r2_elastic = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_elastic)

# Print the results
print("Best alpha (Elastic Net):", elastic_cv.alpha_)
print("Best l1_ratio:", elastic_cv.l1_ratio_)
print(f"Elastic Net MSE (on test data): {mse_elastic:.2f}")
print(f"Elastic Net RMSE (on test data): {rmse_elastic:.2f}")
print(f"Elastic Net R² Score: {r2_elastic:.4f}")
```

        i. Apply a log transformation of the target, `price`, the Lasso Regression (L1) technique to the MLR model. 

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from ISLP.models import (ModelSpec as MS,
                         summarize,
                         poly)
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Add constant (column: intercept=1) column into X
design = MS(X.columns)
X = design.fit_transform(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)
```

```{python}
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso

# Step 2: Get a lambda grid given length of lambda and min_ratio of lambda_max
def get_lambda_l1(xs: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray, nlambda: int, min_ratio: float):
    ybar = np.mean(y)
    xbar = np.mean(xs, axis=0)
    xs_centered = xs - xbar
    xty = np.dot(xs_centered.T, (y - ybar))
    lmax = np.max(np.abs(xty))
    lambdas = np.logspace(np.log10(lmax), np.log10(min_ratio * lmax),
                              num=nlambda)
    return lambdas

# Step 3: Calculate lambda values
nlambda = 100
min_ratio = 1e-20
lambda_values = get_lambda_l1(X, y, nlambda, min_ratio)

# Step 5: Initialize arrays to store the coefficients and MSE for each lambda value
coefficients = []
mse_list = []

# Step 6: Fit multiple linear regression with L1 regularization (Lasso) for each lambda value
for lmbda in lambda_values:
    lasso = Lasso(alpha=lmbda, max_iter=10000)
    lasso.fit(X_train, y_train)
    
    # Store coefficients
    coefficients.append(lasso.coef_)
    
    # Predict and calculate MSE
    y_pred = lasso.predict(X_test)
    mse = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred)
    mse_list.append(mse)

# Step 7: Convert results to DataFrame
coef_df = pd.DataFrame(coefficients, columns=X.columns)
mse_series = pd.Series(mse_list, index=lambda_values)

# Convert list of coefficient arrays to a NumPy array
coefficients = np.array(coefficients)

# Step 7: Plot the solution path for each feature
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
for i in range(coefficients.shape[1]):
    plt.plot(lambda_values, coefficients[:, i], label=f'Feature {i + 1}')
    
plt.xscale('log')
plt.xlabel('Lambda values (log scale)')
plt.ylabel('Coefficient value')
plt.title('Solution Path of MLR Lasso Regression')
plt.grid(True)
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left', fontsize='small')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# Find the best lambda with the lowest MSE
best_idx = np.argmin(mse_list)
best_lambda = lambda_values[best_idx]
best_coef = coefficients[best_idx]

print(f"Best lambda: {best_lambda:.4f}")

# Refit the MLR model with the best lambda on the training data
lasso_best = Lasso(alpha=best_lambda, max_iter=10000)
lasso_best.fit(X_train, y_train)

# Predict on test set
y_pred_best = lasso_best.predict(X_test)

# Compute MSE, RMSE and R^2 score
mse_best = mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred_best)
rmse_best = np.sqrt(mse_best)
r2_best = r2_score(y_test, y_pred_best)

# Print the results
print(f"MSE with best lambda (on test data): {mse_best:.2f}")
print(f"RMSE with best lambda (on test data): {rmse_best:.2f}")
print(f"R² Score with best lambda: {r2_best:.4f}")
```

Conclusion:
By applying the Ridge Regression (L2), the Elastic net Regression (L1 + L2), and also the Lasso Regression (L1), regularisation techniques, the model has not been improved. Therefore, we try to Drop One of the Correlated Predictor from all of the Highly Correlated Predictor pairs.

        i. Apply a log transformation of the target, `price` and Drop One of the Correlated Predictor from all of the Highly Correlated Predictor pairs.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
import statsmodels.api as sm
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, r2_score
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

# Sample selection of columns
df_XGBoot_model = df_2024_merged.drop(columns=['latitude', 'longitude', 'zip_code'])

# Check the dimension of the dataset, df_XGBoot_model
print(df_XGBoot_model.shape)

# Split the dataset into features (X) and target (y)
X = df_XGBoot_model.drop(columns=['price'])  # Features
y = np.log1p(df_XGBoot_model['price'])  # Target variable

# List all of the numerical variables
numerical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if df_XGBoot_model[column].dtypes != 'object']

# List all of the categorical variables
categorical_cols = [column for column in X.columns if column not in numerical_cols]

# Use onehot_encoding to transform the categorical variable
X = pd.get_dummies(X, columns = categorical_cols, drop_first=True)
X.replace({True:1, False:0}, inplace=True)

# Standardize *only* numerical columns (keep dummy variables untouched)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X[numerical_cols] = scaler.fit_transform(X[numerical_cols])

# Add intercept for OLS Regression
X = sm.add_constant(X)

# Split the data into training and testing sets.
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.2, random_state=1234)

# Fit/train the model on 80% training data
MLR_model = sm.OLS(y_train, X_train).fit()
```

```{python}
# Print full summary of the MLR_model result
print(MLR_model.summary())

# Predict 'price' on the 20% test data
```
